text.fullText: From: Ann Woll <twoll@mit.edu> To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 1/30/2026 5:26:49 PM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 ------------------------------------------ You don't often get email from twoll@mit.edu. Learn why this is important Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. The Environmental Protection Agency, I am asking you to drop the proposed changes to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. Clean water is necessary for health - it's that simple. I am eighty-one years old and have two children and three granddaughters. For all of us, including you at the EPA, don't our families deserve to have clean water to drink and cook with? Don't they deserve the luxury of turning on the tap and not having to fear pollutants or disease? For 50 years, Section 401 of the Clean Water Act has provided essential protection for our nation's waterways by giving states and authorized tribes the ability to review federally permitted projects that could affect their water quality. The corporations don't care about the health of the people who live near their projects. Corporations' job is to make as much money as possible. It is the governments' job to represent and protect the public. And the public is your children and ours. The proposed rule changes would significantly limit any authority's ability to protect the public by restricting reviews to only "direct discharges" into waterways, rather than allowing comprehensive assessments of broader environmental impacts. This change would weaken local communities' ability to protect drinking water sources, fisheries, wetlands, and cultural resources. Furthermore, the proposed strict deadlines and limited review scope would accelerate project approvals without ensuring adequate review for the protection of the environment. This comes at a particularly concerning time, following recent judicial decisions that have already reduced federal water protections. Water is a shared resource that sustains life in communities, ecosystems, and economies. Once contaminated, restoration can take generations. The current Section 401 authority provides essential safeguards that allow local communities to protect their water resources for current and future generations. I urge the EPA to withdraw this proposed rule and maintain the current authority that allows states and tribes to conduct meaningful water quality reviews. The people of the United States, your families and ours, deserve comprehensive protection, not weakened standards that prioritize expedited project approvals over our health and safety. Sincerely, Ann Massachusetts
